title: Aquatic Wind Tunnel: Drag Coefficient Simulator
description: Users operate a fluid dynamics testing facility where a BarramundiFish and a CarConcept are placed inside a virtual wind tunnel. By adjusting fluid density, flow velocity, object orientation, and cross-sectional reference area, users compare aerodynamic vs hydrodynamic drag forces in real-time, observing particle streams deforming around each shape and force vectors updating live.
prompt:
Create a single HTML file using Three.js r170 with GLTFLoader and OrbitControls from CDN. Load BarramundiFish.glb and CarConcept.glb side by side inside a translucent rectangular wind tunnel (semi-transparent box geometry with wireframe edges). Scale the fish to roughly 1.5m length and the car to roughly 4m length, both centered vertically on pedestals inside the tunnel. Implement a particle system (500+ small spheres or points) flowing from left to right through the tunnel, simulating fluid flow. Particles should deflect around each object using simplified potential flow approximation: particles closer to objects curve around them based on proximity. Add visible force arrow helpers (ArrowHelper) on each object showing the computed drag force vector. The drag formula is F = 0.5 * rho * v^2 * Cd * A. Provide an HTML control panel with: (1) a slider for fluid density rho (0.1 to 1025 kg/m^3, default 1.225 for air), (2) a slider for flow velocity v (1 to 100 m/s, default 20), (3) a dropdown to select which object to rotate (fish or car), (4) a rotation slider for yaw angle (-90 to 90 degrees) that rotates the selected object and recalculates effective cross-sectional area A, (5) a numeric input to override Cd for each object (fish default 0.04, car default 0.28), (6) a toggle button switching particle color between blue (water mode) and white (air mode). Display computed drag force in Newtons for both objects in a live readout panel. Validate all inputs: density must be positive, velocity 1-100, Cd between 0.01 and 2.0, yaw between -90 and 90. Camera starts at a 30-degree elevated angle looking at the tunnel center. Use ambient light plus two directional lights for clear visibility. The tunnel floor should have a subtle grid texture. Particle speed should visually match the velocity slider. Arrow lengths should scale proportionally to computed force magnitude.

[State Exposure (required)]
Expose window.__3D_STATE__ updated every animation frame:
window.__3D_STATE__ = {
  fluidDensity: number,         // current fluid density in kg/m³ (0.1 to 1025)
  flowVelocity: number,         // flow velocity in m/s (1 to 100)
  fishYawAngle: number,         // fish rotation angle in degrees (-90 to 90)
  carYawAngle: number,         // car rotation angle in degrees (-90 to 90)
  fishDragForce: number,         // computed drag force on fish in Newtons
  carDragForce: number,         // computed drag force on car in Newtons
  particleCount: number,         // number of active flow particles in tunnel
  selectedObject: string,         // currently selected object for rotation ('fish' or 'car')
  fishCrossSectionalArea: number,         // effective cross-sectional area of fish in m²
  carCrossSectionalArea: number,         // effective cross-sectional area of car in m²
  simulationRunning: boolean,         // whether particle flow simulation is active
  tunnelVisible: boolean,         // whether wind tunnel walls are visible
}
